boardPro.name: + Board Pro
boardPro.description: Advanced views, automation, per-card financials, and executive reporting.

workflowGuide.title: How Billing Works
workflowGuide.subtitle: Project to invoice in {count} steps
workflowGuide.s1desc: Each board represents one project. Link it to a client so all billing and invoices stay connected.
workflowGuide.s1example: Example: "Acme Corp Website Redesign" board, linked to client Acme Corp.
workflowGuide.s2desc: Each list can become a billing milestone. Set an expected amount, currency, and planned invoice date. This defines when and how much you will bill.
workflowGuide.s2example: Example: "Design Phase" list -- 500 EUR, invoice date March 15.
workflowGuide.s3desc: Add cards (tasks) to each list. Use checklists inside cards for granular tracking. As you complete items, the milestone progress fills up automatically.
workflowGuide.s3example: Example: Card "Create logo" with checklist: Sketches, Color palette, Final version.
workflowGuide.s4desc: With Board Pro, each card can have its own revenue and cost estimate. This gives you a per-task profit margin breakdown beyond just milestone totals.
workflowGuide.s4example: Example: Card "Frontend build" -- Revenue: 800 EUR, Cost: 400 EUR, Margin: 400 EUR.
workflowGuide.s5desc: When all tasks in a milestone are done, it shows "Ready to invoice". Open the card's Invoice panel to create a CRM invoice directly -- or use automation.
workflowGuide.s5example: Example: "Design Phase" hits 100% -> click "Create Invoice" -> draft invoice for 500 EUR appears in CRM.
workflowGuide.s6desc: All invoices live in CRM Pro. Review the draft, add line items, send it to the client, record payments, or push it to Billingo / Szamlazz.hu for official invoicing.
workflowGuide.s6example: Example: Invoice #2024-015 for Acme Corp -- 500 EUR -- Status: Sent -- Due: April 15.
workflowGuide.s7desc: Set up a CRM automation rule: when a board is closed, automatically create a draft invoice. No manual steps needed -- close the project, invoice appears.
workflowGuide.s7example: Example: Rule "Auto-invoice on close" -- Trigger: Board closed -> Action: Create invoice draft.

emailRulesGuide.title: How Email Rules Work
emailRulesGuide.subtitle: Email-to-board routing in {count} steps
emailRulesGuide.s1desc: Email rules automatically link incoming emails to your board. When an email matches your criteria, it gets attached to an existing card or creates a new one.
emailRulesGuide.s1example: Example: Every email from "client.com" automatically appears as a card on your project board.
emailRulesGuide.s2desc: Choose how to match incoming emails. You can match by subject line keywords, sender domain, specific sender email, or email label/folder.
emailRulesGuide.s2example: Match types: "Subject contains [Project X]", "Sender domain = client.com", "Sender email = boss@work.com".
emailRulesGuide.s3desc: When "Auto-create card" is enabled, a matching email creates a new card on the board. The email subject becomes the card title, and the email body is attached.
emailRulesGuide.s3example: Example: Email with subject "Logo feedback" from client.com -> New card "Logo feedback" in the "Inbox" list.
emailRulesGuide.s4desc: If auto-create is off, matching emails are linked to the most relevant existing card (matched by subject similarity). This keeps email threads organized under the right task.
emailRulesGuide.s4example: Example: Reply email "Re: Logo feedback" links to the existing "Logo feedback" card instead of creating a duplicate.
emailRulesGuide.s5desc: Optionally auto-assign matching cards to a specific team member. Combined with the target list setting, you can route different email types to different people and workflows.
emailRulesGuide.s5example: Example: Emails from "support@" -> List "Support Tickets" -> Assigned to team@company.com.
emailRulesGuide.s6desc: A common setup: create one rule per client domain. Emails from acme.com go to the "Acme Corp" board, emails from beta.io go to the "Beta Project" board.
emailRulesGuide.s6example: Rule 1: Sender domain "acme.com" -> Create card in "Incoming". Rule 2: Subject contains "[urgent]" -> Create card in "Priority" list.
emailRulesGuide.s7desc: Toggle rules on and off without deleting them. This is useful when a project wraps up -- just disable the rule. Re-enable it later if work resumes.
emailRulesGuide.s7example: Tip: Start with a broad rule (sender domain), then add specific ones (subject keywords) as your workflow evolves.

automationGuide.title: How Automations Work
automationGuide.subtitle: Trigger-action rules in {count} steps
automationGuide.s1desc: Automations are "if this, then that" rules for your board. When something happens (a trigger), an action runs automatically. No manual work needed.
automationGuide.s1example: Example: When a card moves to the "Done" list, automatically notify the team.
automationGuide.s2desc: A trigger is the event that starts the automation. You can react to card movements, completions, overdue deadlines, idle cards, new cards, labels, checklists, or incoming emails.
automationGuide.s2example: Common triggers: "Card moved to list", "Card is overdue", "Checklist completed", "Email received on card".
automationGuide.s3desc: An action is what happens when the trigger fires. Move cards, assign members, add labels, send notifications, create invoice drafts, post chat messages, or update CRM deal stages.
automationGuide.s3example: Common actions: "Move card to Done", "Send notification", "Create invoice draft", "Post chat message".
automationGuide.s4desc: Create a rule: when a card's checklist is fully completed, move it to the "Done" list automatically. Great for keeping the board clean.
automationGuide.s4example: Rule: "Auto-archive done" -- Trigger: Checklist completed -> Action: Move card to "Done".
automationGuide.s5desc: Set up a rule that fires when a card passes its due date. The action sends a notification or chat message so nothing falls through the cracks.
automationGuide.s5example: Rule: "Overdue alert" -- Trigger: Card is overdue -> Action: Send notification.
automationGuide.s6desc: When all cards in a milestone list are completed, automatically create an invoice draft in CRM. The milestone amount and client are pulled from the board settings.
automationGuide.s6example: Rule: "Invoice on milestone" -- Trigger: All cards done in list -> Action: Create invoice draft.
automationGuide.s7desc: Each rule has a toggle to enable/disable it without deleting. You can see how many times a rule has fired (run count). Create as many rules as you need per board.
automationGuide.s7example: Tip: Start with one simple rule (like overdue alerts), see it work, then add more.

crmSequencesGuide.title: How Email Sequences Work
crmSequencesGuide.subtitle: Drip campaigns in {count} steps
crmSequencesGuide.s1desc: Email sequences are automated drip campaigns. You define a series of emails with delays between them. Once a contact is enrolled, the system sends each email on schedule.
crmSequencesGuide.s1example: Example: A 3-email welcome sequence -- Day 0: "Welcome!", Day 3: "Getting started guide", Day 7: "How can we help?".
crmSequencesGuide.s2desc: Give your sequence a name (e.g. "Welcome Series"). Then add steps -- each step is an email with a subject, body, and delay in days from the previous step.
crmSequencesGuide.s2example: Step 1 (Day 0): "Thanks for your interest!" -> Step 2 (Day 2): "Here's what we offer" -> Step 3 (Day 5): "Ready to get started?".
crmSequencesGuide.s3desc: You can enroll contacts manually from the Sequences view, or automatically via CRM Automation rules. Each enrollment tracks its progress independently.
crmSequencesGuide.s3example: Manual: Click "Enroll" on a sequence, type the contact email. Automatic: Create an automation rule with action "Start email sequence".
crmSequencesGuide.s4desc: Each enrolled contact shows their current step, status (active, completed, cancelled), and next scheduled send. You can cancel an enrollment at any time.
crmSequencesGuide.s4example: In the enrollments panel: "john@client.com -- Step 2 of 3 -- Next email in 2 days -- Status: Active".
crmSequencesGuide.s5desc: After sending a proposal, enroll the client in a follow-up sequence: Day 2: "Just checking in", Day 5: "Happy to answer questions", Day 10: "Would you like to schedule a call?".
crmSequencesGuide.s5example: Combine with automation: Trigger "Deal stage changed to Proposal" -> Action "Start sequence: Proposal Follow-up".
crmSequencesGuide.s6desc: When a deal is won, start an onboarding sequence: Day 0: "Welcome aboard!", Day 1: "Your setup guide", Day 3: "Meet your account manager", Day 7: "Check-in".
crmSequencesGuide.s6example: Automation trigger: "Deal won" -> Action: "Start sequence: Client Onboarding". All touchpoints happen automatically.
crmSequencesGuide.s7desc: Keep sequences short (3-5 emails max). Use clear subjects. Space emails 2-5 days apart. Always include value in each email. Test sequences on yourself first.
crmSequencesGuide.s7example: Tip: Create a few standard sequences (Welcome, Follow-up, Onboarding) and connect them to automation rules.

crmAutomationGuide.title: How CRM Automations Work
crmAutomationGuide.subtitle: Trigger-action rules in {count} steps
crmAutomationGuide.s1desc: CRM automations are "if this happens, then do that" rules for your sales pipeline, clients, and invoices. They run automatically when a trigger condition is met.
crmAutomationGuide.s1example: Example: When a deal has been sitting in "Negotiation" for 7+ days, automatically send a follow-up reminder.
crmAutomationGuide.s2desc: React to changes in your sales pipeline. Triggers fire when deals move between stages, sit idle too long, are won, or are lost.
crmAutomationGuide.s2example: Available: "Deal stage changed", "Deal idle in stage" (e.g. 7+ days), "Deal won", "Deal lost".
crmAutomationGuide.s3desc: Monitor client health and invoice status. Get alerted when a client's health score drops, when there's been no contact for too long, or when an invoice becomes overdue.
crmAutomationGuide.s3example: Available: "Client health low", "No contact for X days", "Invoice overdue".
crmAutomationGuide.s4desc: CRM automation can react to events from other features: when a board is closed, tracked time reaches a threshold, or drive folder permissions change.
crmAutomationGuide.s4example: Available: "Board closed", "Time spent reached", "Drive folder permissions changed".
crmAutomationGuide.s5desc: When a trigger fires, one of these actions runs: send an email, create an invoice draft, move a deal, notify a user, start an email sequence, or send a chat message.
crmAutomationGuide.s5example: Common combo: "Invoice overdue by 3 days" triggers "Send email" to the client with a payment reminder.
crmAutomationGuide.s6desc: When a deal is marked as won, automatically create an invoice draft with the deal's value and client. The draft appears in your Invoices tab ready for review.
crmAutomationGuide.s6example: Rule: "Invoice on close" -- Trigger: Deal won -> Action: Create invoice draft.
crmAutomationGuide.s7desc: If a deal sits in "Proposal" for more than 5 days, automatically send a follow-up email to the client or notify your sales team via chat.
crmAutomationGuide.s7example: Rule: "Stale deal nudge" -- Trigger: Deal idle in "Proposal" for 5+ days -> Action: Send follow-up email.
crmAutomationGuide.s8desc: When a deal enters your pipeline (stage changes to "Lead"), automatically start a welcome email sequence to nurture the lead.
crmAutomationGuide.s8example: Rule: "Welcome sequence" -- Trigger: Deal stage changed to "Lead" -> Action: Start email sequence "Welcome Series".
crmAutomationGuide.s9desc: Toggle rules on/off without deleting them. Share rules with colleagues. Test-fire a rule to verify it works. Check the Execution Log to see what ran and when.
crmAutomationGuide.s9example: Tip: Start with one rule (like "overdue invoice reminder"), verify in the Execution Log, then expand.

drive.title: How Drive Works
drive.subtitle: File management in {count} steps
drive.s1desc: Upload files from your computer or save attachments from emails directly to your personal drive. Every file is stored securely and accessible from anywhere.
drive.s1example: Example: Drag a contract PDF into your Drive -- it's instantly available across all your devices.
drive.s2desc: Create nested folder structures to keep your projects tidy. Move files between folders, rename them, and keep everything where you can find it.
drive.s2example: Example: Create a "Projects / Acme Corp / Contracts" folder hierarchy for a client's documents.
drive.s3desc: Share folders with colleagues and set permissions. Collaborators can view or edit files depending on the access level you grant them.
drive.s3example: Example: Share the "Design Assets" folder with your design team as editors so they can upload their work.
drive.s4desc: Every time a file is updated, Drive keeps the previous version. You can browse the version history and restore any earlier version if needed.
drive.s4example: Example: You overwrite a logo file -- no problem, open version history and restore the previous version.
drive.s5desc: Files from Drive can be attached to board cards, sent in chat messages, and linked to client records. Drive is the central file hub for the entire app.
drive.s5example: Example: Attach a wireframe from Drive to a board card, then share the same file in a chat channel.

clients.title: How Clients Work
clients.subtitle: Client management in {count} steps
clients.s1desc: Clients are auto-detected from email domains. When you email someone at acme.com, a client record for Acme is created automatically. You can also add clients manually.
clients.s1example: Example: Email john{'@'}acme.com and the system auto-creates "acme.com" as a client with John as the first contact.
clients.s2desc: Each client can have multiple contacts -- people you communicate with at that company. Each contact has a name, email, phone, and position.
clients.s2example: Example: Acme Corp has 3 contacts: John (CEO), Jane (CTO), and Bob (Designer).
clients.s3desc: Set up billing details including company name, tax number, billing address, bank account, and payment terms. Used when generating invoices.
clients.s3example: Example: Set Acme's payment terms to 30 days and add their tax number for automatic invoice generation.
clients.s4desc: Connect clients to project boards. When a board is linked to a client, all milestones, time tracking, and invoices are associated with that client.
clients.s4example: Example: Link the "Website Redesign" board to Acme Corp -- all billable work flows to their account.
clients.s5desc: See a unified view of all activity for each client: time tracked, tasks completed, invoices sent, and total revenue. Everything in one place.
clients.s5example: Example: Open Acme's overview to see 45 hours tracked this month, 3 open tasks, and $12,000 invoiced.

calendar.title: How Calendar Works
calendar.subtitle: Scheduling in {count} steps
calendar.s1desc: Add events with a title, date, time, location, and description. Events appear on your personal calendar and can be viewed by day, week, or month.
calendar.s1example: Example: Create a "Team Standup" event for Monday at 9:00 AM in the meeting room.
calendar.s2desc: Invite colleagues to meetings. Add video call links, set the location, and everyone sees it on their calendar with notifications.
calendar.s2example: Example: Schedule a design review with 3 team members and attach a video call link.
calendar.s3desc: Set reminders before events so you never miss a meeting. Get notified by browser notification or email at the time you choose.
calendar.s3example: Example: Set a 15-minute reminder for client calls and a 1-hour reminder for preparation-heavy meetings.
calendar.s4desc: Create events that repeat daily, weekly, monthly, or on custom schedules. Edit one occurrence or the entire series.
calendar.s4example: Example: Set up a weekly Monday standup that repeats every week indefinitely.
calendar.s5desc: Calendar connects with boards (due dates appear as events), time tracking (log time from events), and tasks (deadlines sync both ways).
calendar.s5example: Example: A board card due date automatically shows on your calendar. Start time tracking directly from the event.

timeTracker.title: How Time Tracking Works
timeTracker.subtitle: Track time in {count} steps
timeTracker.s1desc: Click the play button to start a timer. Add a description of what you're working on. Stop the timer when you're done -- the entry is saved automatically.
timeTracker.s1example: Example: Click play, type "Frontend development for Acme homepage", work for 2 hours, then stop.
timeTracker.s2desc: Associate each time entry with a specific client. This lets you track how much time you spend on each client's projects for accurate billing.
timeTracker.s2example: Example: Log 3 hours of design work under "Acme Corp" -- it appears in their client overview.
timeTracker.s3desc: Categorize time entries by activity type: development, design, meetings, admin, support, and more. Helps you understand where your time goes.
timeTracker.s3example: Example: This week: 20h development, 5h meetings, 3h design -- see where your time is actually spent.
timeTracker.s4desc: View detailed time reports by day, week, month, or custom range. Filter by client, project, or activity type to get the exact breakdown you need.
timeTracker.s4example: Example: Generate a monthly report showing all time tracked for Acme Corp, grouped by activity type.
timeTracker.s5desc: Time entries feed directly into billing calculations. Set hourly rates per client, and the system calculates totals automatically for invoice generation.
timeTracker.s5example: Example: 10 hours at $150/hr for Acme = $1,500 automatically calculated and ready for invoicing.

moodBoard.title: How Mood Boards Work
moodBoard.subtitle: Visual design in {count} steps
moodBoard.s1desc: Start with a blank canvas or choose from templates. Each mood board is an infinite canvas where you can arrange visual content freely.
moodBoard.s1example: Example: Create a "Brand Identity" mood board to collect colors, typography, and inspiration images.
moodBoard.s2desc: Drop images, text blocks, shapes, drawings, and audio onto your canvas. Import files from Drive or upload directly. Resize and position everything freely.
moodBoard.s2example: Example: Add a hero image, a color palette swatch, and text notes describing the design direction.
moodBoard.s3desc: Link related items with visual connections. Group items together, use frames to organize sections, and align everything with guides.
moodBoard.s3example: Example: Connect the "Logo" item to "Color Palette" and "Typography" with arrows showing the relationship.
moodBoard.s4desc: Switch to presentation mode to walk through your mood board slide by slide. Create artboards as slides and present them fullscreen.
moodBoard.s4example: Example: Create 5 artboard slides and present the brand concept to your client in fullscreen mode.
moodBoard.s5desc: Share mood boards with a public link for external review, or invite team members to collaborate. Export as images for offline use.
moodBoard.s5example: Example: Send a public link to your client so they can review the mood board and leave feedback.

team.title: How Team Management Works
team.subtitle: Team setup in {count} steps
team.s1desc: Invite team members by their email address. They'll get access to shared resources based on their role. Manage your entire team from one place.
team.s1example: Example: Add jane{'@'}company.com as a colleague -- she can now access shared boards, chat, and Drive folders.
team.s2desc: Assign roles to control what colleagues can access. Admins have full control, editors can modify content, and viewers have read-only access.
team.s2example: Example: Set your project manager as Admin, developers as Editors, and the client contact as Viewer.
team.s3desc: Organize colleagues into departments like Design, Development, or Marketing. Departments help with filtering and bulk permissions.
team.s3example: Example: Create a "Development" department and assign all your developers -- now share boards to the whole group.
team.s4desc: Colleagues automatically get access to resources shared with them: boards, Drive folders, chat channels, and calendar events.
team.s4example: Example: When you share a board with a colleague, they see it in their sidebar and get notifications for changes.
team.s5desc: Track team activity and manage security settings. See who accessed what, review login history, and revoke access when needed.
team.s5example: Example: Check the activity log to see who edited the project board last week.

chat.title: How Chat Works
chat.subtitle: Communication in {count} steps
chat.s1desc: Send private 1:1 messages to any colleague. Share text, files, images, and links. Messages are instant and encrypted.
chat.s1example: Example: Send a quick message to your designer: "The new mockup looks great, let's discuss the color palette."
chat.s2desc: Create topic-based channels for team discussions. Channels can be public (anyone can join) or private (invite-only). Pin important messages.
chat.s2example: Example: Create a #design-reviews channel where the team discusses and approves design work.
chat.s3desc: Share files, images, and documents directly in conversations. Files are saved to Drive automatically. Preview images and documents inline.
chat.s3example: Example: Drop a wireframe image into the #design channel -- everyone sees it inline and can download it.
chat.s4desc: Start voice or video calls directly from any chat. Screen share during calls. Huddles are lightweight audio calls for quick discussions.
chat.s4example: Example: Start a huddle in the #development channel for a quick 5-minute sync without scheduling a meeting.
chat.s5desc: Chat connects with boards (get notifications for card changes), tasks (create tasks from messages), and other modules for a unified workflow.
chat.s5example: Example: Get a chat notification when a board card is assigned to you, and reply with a status update.

automationHub.title: How Automation Hub Works
automationHub.subtitle: Automation in {count} steps
automationHub.s1desc: Build automated workflows by connecting triggers to actions. When something happens (trigger), the system automatically performs the actions you defined.
automationHub.s1example: Example: When a new email arrives from a VIP client -> Create a high-priority task on the board.
automationHub.s2desc: Triggers are events that start your workflow: incoming emails, form submissions, scheduled times, webhook calls, or manual activation.
automationHub.s2example: Example: Trigger on "New email from support{'@'}" to auto-create support tickets.
automationHub.s3desc: Actions are what happens when the workflow triggers: send emails, create board cards, update CRM deals, post chat messages, or call external APIs.
automationHub.s3example: Example: Action chain: Send acknowledgment email -> Create board card -> Notify team in chat.
automationHub.s4desc: Add conditions to route workflows differently based on data. If/else branches let you handle different scenarios in a single workflow.
automationHub.s4example: Example: If deal value > $10,000 -> Notify manager. Else -> Assign to sales rep automatically.
automationHub.s5desc: Check the execution log to see every workflow run, its status, and any errors. Debug failed runs and optimize your automations over time.
automationHub.s5example: Example: Execution log shows your "Welcome Email" workflow ran 50 times this week with 100% success rate.

mailingLists.title: How Mailing Lists Work
mailingLists.subtitle: List management in {count} steps
mailingLists.s1desc: Set up subscriber lists for different audiences. Each list has a name, description, and its own set of subscribers that you can target in campaigns.
mailingLists.s1example: Example: Create lists for "Newsletter Subscribers", "Product Updates", and "Beta Testers".
mailingLists.s2desc: Import subscribers from CSV files, add them manually, or let them subscribe through forms. Each subscriber has an email, name, and custom fields.
mailingLists.s2example: Example: Import 500 newsletter subscribers from a CSV file with name, email, and company columns.
mailingLists.s3desc: Segment your lists based on subscriber data: location, signup date, engagement level, or custom fields. Send targeted campaigns to specific segments.
mailingLists.s3example: Example: Create a segment of subscribers who opened the last 3 emails -- your most engaged audience.
mailingLists.s4desc: Track subscriber status: active, unsubscribed, bounced. Clean your lists automatically. Handle unsubscribes and bounces gracefully.
mailingLists.s4example: Example: The system auto-removes bounced emails and processes unsubscribe requests instantly.
mailingLists.s5desc: Select one or more mailing lists when creating email campaigns. Combine lists and segments for precise targeting.
mailingLists.s5example: Example: Send a product launch campaign to "Beta Testers" + "Newsletter" lists, excluding unengaged subscribers.

campaigns.title: How Email Campaigns Work
campaigns.subtitle: Email marketing in {count} steps
campaigns.s1desc: Start a new email campaign by giving it a name and choosing the type: newsletter, product update, announcement, or custom. Save drafts to finish later.
campaigns.s1example: Example: Create a "February Newsletter" campaign to share company updates with your subscribers.
campaigns.s2desc: Use the visual email editor to craft your message. Add text, images, buttons, and links. Preview how it looks on desktop and mobile.
campaigns.s2example: Example: Design a newsletter with a hero image, 3 article summaries, and a call-to-action button.
campaigns.s3desc: Choose which mailing lists and segments receive the campaign. Combine multiple lists, exclude segments, and see the estimated reach.
campaigns.s3example: Example: Send to "All Subscribers" minus "Unengaged" segment = 2,340 recipients.
campaigns.s4desc: Send the campaign immediately or schedule it for a specific date and time. Choose the best sending time for your audience's timezone.
campaigns.s4example: Example: Schedule the newsletter for Tuesday at 10:00 AM when open rates are highest.
campaigns.s5desc: Monitor campaign performance in real-time: open rate, click rate, bounces, and unsubscribes. See which links were clicked most and by whom.
campaigns.s5example: Example: Your newsletter had a 35% open rate and 12% click rate -- the product link got the most clicks.

crmPipeline.title: How CRM Pipeline Works
crmPipeline.subtitle: Sales pipeline in {count} steps
crmPipeline.s1desc: Set up sales pipelines with custom stages that match your sales process. Each pipeline has a name and ordered stages that deals move through.
crmPipeline.s1example: Example: Create a "Sales Pipeline" with stages: Lead -> Qualified -> Proposal -> Negotiation -> Closed.
crmPipeline.s2desc: Create deals with a name, value, contact, and expected close date. Assign deals to team members and track them as they progress through stages.
crmPipeline.s2example: Example: Add deal "Acme Website Redesign" worth $15,000, linked to contact John at Acme Corp.
crmPipeline.s3desc: Drag deals between stages as they progress. Each stage change is tracked with a timestamp so you can analyze how long deals spend in each stage.
crmPipeline.s3example: Example: Drag the Acme deal from "Proposal" to "Negotiation" -- the system logs the stage change.
crmPipeline.s4desc: See projected revenue based on deal values and stages. Weight deals by probability to get a realistic forecast of expected income.
crmPipeline.s4example: Example: Pipeline shows $50K in proposals (50% probability) + $30K in negotiation (80%) = $49K weighted forecast.
crmPipeline.s5desc: Mark deals as won or lost with reasons. Track win rates, average deal size, and time-to-close. Use insights to improve your sales process.
crmPipeline.s5example: Example: This quarter: 15 deals won ($120K), 5 lost -- win rate 75%, average time-to-close 23 days.

crmInvoices.title: How CRM Invoices Work
crmInvoices.subtitle: Invoicing in {count} steps
crmInvoices.s1desc: Generate invoices with line items, quantities, rates, and tax. Invoices are calculated automatically with subtotals and totals.
crmInvoices.s1example: Example: Create an invoice for Acme with 3 line items: design (10h x $100), development (20h x $120), hosting ($50/mo).
crmInvoices.s2desc: Associate invoices with clients to auto-fill billing info: company name, address, tax number, and payment terms. All stored from the client record.
crmInvoices.s2example: Example: Select "Acme Corp" and billing name, address, and tax number are filled automatically.
crmInvoices.s3desc: Email invoices directly to clients or export as PDF for manual sending. The system tracks when an invoice was sent and viewed.
crmInvoices.s3example: Example: Click Send and the invoice PDF is emailed to Acme's billing contact with a professional template.
crmInvoices.s4desc: Monitor invoice lifecycle: Draft -> Sent -> Paid -> Overdue. Get notified when invoices become overdue. Filter by status for quick overview.
crmInvoices.s4example: Example: Dashboard shows 5 paid ($25K), 3 sent ($12K), 1 overdue ($3K) -- follow up on the overdue one.
crmInvoices.s5desc: Connect to Billingo or Szamlazz.hu for automated invoice generation with legal compliance. Invoices sync both ways between the systems.
crmInvoices.s5example: Example: Create an invoice here and it's automatically generated in Billingo with the correct Hungarian tax format.

crmDashboard.title: How CRM Dashboard Works
crmDashboard.subtitle: CRM overview in {count} steps
crmDashboard.s1desc: See all your pipelines at a glance: total deal count, total value, and deals per stage. Quickly identify which stages need attention.
crmDashboard.s1example: Example: Dashboard shows 25 active deals worth $180K across 3 pipelines, with 8 deals stuck in "Proposal".
crmDashboard.s2desc: Track key metrics: total revenue, won deals this period, average deal size, and pipeline velocity. Compare across time periods to spot trends.
crmDashboard.s2example: Example: This month: $45K won (up 20% from last month), 12 deals closed, avg size $3,750.
crmDashboard.s3desc: See recent CRM activity across your entire team: new deals, stage changes, invoices sent, and payments received. Stay on top of everything.
crmDashboard.s3example: Example: Feed shows Jane moved 3 deals to "Won" today, and 2 invoices were paid this morning.
crmDashboard.s4desc: Visualize your sales data with charts: revenue over time, deals by stage, win/loss ratios, and team performance comparisons.
crmDashboard.s4example: Example: The revenue trend chart shows steady growth from $30K/month to $50K/month over the last quarter.
crmDashboard.s5desc: Jump directly to deals, invoices, or contacts from the dashboard. Create new deals, send invoices, or update deal stages without leaving the overview.
crmDashboard.s5example: Example: Spot a stale deal on the dashboard, click it, update the stage, and add a note -- all in seconds.

boards.title: How Boards Work
boards.subtitle: Project management in {count} steps
boards.s1desc: Boards are your project workspaces. Each board has lists (columns) that represent stages of work. Start with a blank board or use the default setup.
boards.s1example: Example: Create a "Website Redesign" board with lists: Backlog, In Progress, Review, Done.
boards.s2desc: Cards represent tasks or work items. Add descriptions, checklists, due dates, and attachments. Cards live in lists and can be moved between them.
boards.s2example: Example: Create a "Design Homepage" card with a checklist: wireframe, mockup, review, implement.
boards.s3desc: Color-code cards with labels to categorize them (bug, feature, urgent). Filter and search cards by label, assignee, due date, or text.
boards.s3example: Example: Label cards as "Bug" (red), "Feature" (green), "Urgent" (orange) -- then filter to see only bugs.
boards.s4desc: Move cards between lists by dragging them. This visual workflow makes it easy to see what's in progress, what's blocked, and what's done.
boards.s4example: Example: Drag "Design Homepage" from "In Progress" to "Review" when the mockup is ready for feedback.
boards.s5desc: Invite members to boards, assign cards to specific people, add comments for discussion, and attach files from Drive. Everyone stays in sync.
boards.s5example: Example: Assign the homepage card to Jane, she comments with questions, and you attach the mockup from Drive.
